item.title: I stopped treating my Raspberry Pi like a tiny server — and it finally clicked
item.itunes:title: I stopped treating my Raspberry Pi like a tiny server — and it finally clicked
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
For a long time, I treated every Raspberry Pi like a challenge. If it had Ethernet, USB ports, and enough Linux support to boot, I figured it should be doing more. I’d stack services on it, push it into roles better suited to bigger hardware, and then wonder why the experience felt just a little annoying all the time. The Raspberry Pi is flexible enough to encourage that kind of thinking, which is probably why so many of us fall into it.

The Raspberry Pi is at its best when it disappears into the background.

What finally changed for me was realizing that a Raspberry Pi makes much more sense when I stop treating it like a miniature general-purpose server. It works better when it has a clearly defined role, a modest workload, and a setup I’m not constantly tinkering with just because I can. That shift didn’t make the Pi less useful. It actually made it feel more valuable, because I stopped asking it to prove itself and started letting it be good at the jobs it already handles well.

Treating the Raspberry Pi like an appliance works better

One dedicated task gives the Pi room to shine

The Raspberry Pi is at its best when it disappears into the background. That usually happens when it has a single clear responsibility, such as running Pi-hole, Home Assistant, a lightweight dashboard, or a local backup helper. In those roles, it feels dependable in a way that’s easy to appreciate after you’ve spent time coaxing more demanding workloads into behaving. You set it up, give it a little attention, and then it mostly stays out of your way.

That appliance mindset changes how you make decisions before problems even start. You stop obsessing over how many containers you can cram onto one board and start asking whether this is a device you want to trust with a specific job for months at a time. That’s a healthier question because it focuses on reliability rather than bragging rights. A Raspberry Pi can absolutely run a lot of things, but that doesn’t mean it should be the first choice for every server-shaped idea in your home lab.

Use a Raspberry Pi 4 for lighter, always-on jobs like Pi-hole or other simple home lab services, where low fuss matters more than raw speed. Reach for a Raspberry Pi 5 when you want more responsiveness, a better desktop experience, or extra headroom for heavier tasks. The Pi 4 still shines as a stable little appliance, while the Pi 5 is the better fit when the workload starts asking for more.

It also makes the Pi easier to live with when something does go wrong. Troubleshooting a box with one role is much simpler than untangling a board that has become your DNS server, media helper, metrics collector, and test bench all at once. When the device has a narrow purpose, failures are easier to isolate, and fixes are easier to trust. That kind of predictability matters a lot more than squeezing one extra service onto a board just because you found a guide that said you could.

A Raspberry Pi feels best when it stays focused

Stability matters more than squeezing out every last feature

Once I stopped trying to turn the Pi into a tiny do-everything server, I also stopped rebuilding it all the time. That was a bigger quality-of-life improvement than I expected. A Raspberry Pi that exists to do one useful thing doesn’t need to be endlessly optimized, migrated, or benchmarked against hardware that was never in the same class to begin with. It just needs to boot, stay online, and keep doing its job without drama.

That doesn’t mean the Pi is weak or only good for beginner projects. It means its strengths differ from those of a mini PC or an old office desktop. Low power draw, broad community support, tiny size, and a huge ecosystem of accessories all matter more when the Pi is treated like a specialized tool. The board starts to feel thoughtful rather than compromised once you stop forcing it into roles where CPU headroom and storage flexibility matter more than anything else.

There’s also a mental shift when the Pi becomes part of the environment rather than the center of attention. A dedicated Pi for a smart home controller or network utility feels less like a toy and more like infrastructure. You’re not babysitting it just to prove that ARM can do everything. You’re relying on it because it’s good at a narrow set of tasks and doesn’t need to be anything else. That’s when the Raspberry Pi finally starts to feel mature.

There are real reasons people push the Pi harder

The Pi can still do more than critics admit

To be fair, there’s a reason people keep trying to use Raspberry Pis as tiny servers. Modern models are much faster than the old ones, and Linux on ARM is in a much better place than it used to be. For many hobbyists, the Pi is also the hardware they already have on hand, making it the easiest place to test a new service or learn something without buying another machine. That flexibility is one of the platform’s biggest strengths, and it would be silly to pretend otherwise.

There’s also a certain appeal in building a compact, efficient setup around a Raspberry Pi. If you can self-host a few tools, learn Docker, and keep power use low at the same time, that feels like a win. For light services, the Pi often handles the job just fine. Plenty of people have perfectly reasonable results running multiple containers, small web apps, or lightweight automation tools on a single board, especially when expectations are grounded and storage is managed carefully.

And honestly, part of the Raspberry Pi’s charm has always been experimentation. It invites you to try things. That instinct is not the problem. If anything, it’s one of the reasons the platform still matters so much: it lets people tinker, learn, and build without needing enterprise-grade gear or a spare workstation under the desk. The trouble only starts when experimentation becomes the permanent plan, and the board never graduates into a sensible long-term role.

The Pi becomes more useful after expectations settle down

Using the Pi wisely makes it easier to trust

That’s why I still think the appliance mindset is the better one in the long run. A Raspberry Pi can be a fun sandbox, but it becomes genuinely useful when you decide which experiments deserve to become permanent and which ones don’t. Not every successful weekend project should become a cornerstone of your home lab. Sometimes the smartest move is admitting that a task belongs on a mini PC, while the Pi handles something smaller and more predictable.

The nice part is that this shift doesn’t diminish the Raspberry Pi at all. It actually respects what the platform does well, rather than setting you up to be disappointed. When the Pi is given a focused job, it often feels oddly premium despite its limitations. It’s quiet, efficient, simple to place anywhere, and supported by a community that has already solved most of the weird little problems you’re likely to hit.

That’s the moment it finally clicked for me. I stopped seeing the Pi as a tiny server that needed to prove its worth against larger hardware and started seeing it as a home lab appliance that earns its place by being dependable. Since then, my Raspberry Pi setups have felt calmer, cleaner, and much less fragile. I’m doing less on paper with each board, but getting a lot more out of them in practice.

Letting the Raspberry Pi be exactly what it is

What changed my mind wasn’t some dramatic failure or a sudden realization that the Raspberry Pi had been overrated all along. It was the quieter discovery that the board becomes much more satisfying once you stop trying to stretch it into every possible shape. The Raspberry Pi is still one of the most versatile pieces of hardware you can buy for tinkering, learning, and building useful things at home. It just doesn’t need to cosplay as a full server to justify itself.

Treat it like a reliable little appliance instead, and the whole platform starts to make more sense. Give it a job with clear boundaries, let it settle in, and resist the urge to turn every free byte and spare core into a new experiment. There’s still room for curiosity, and there always will be. But the Pi finally clicked for me when I stopped asking how much I could force onto it and started asking what it could do well enough to trust every day.

Raspberry Pi 5
CPU
Arm Cortex-A76 (quad-core, 2.4GHz)
Memory
Up to 8GB LPDDR4X SDRAM

The Raspberry Pi 5 is powerful, but don't let that tempt you into treating it like an all-in-one server.

$92 at Adafruit $85 at CanaKit

item.title: 3 reasons Google's Pixel still feels unlike any other Android phone
item.itunes:title: 3 reasons Google's Pixel still feels unlike any other Android phone
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
When it comes to Android, I've been a Pixel user since the launch of Google's first Pixel smartphone in 2016.

It offers the cleanest take on Android, features excellent camera performance, and, more recently with the Pixel 9 and Pixel 10 series, actually useful AI features. It also remains one of the sleekest devices out there, especially after the recent switch from a visor-style rear camera bump that spans the width of the back of the phone to a cooler-looking oval island.

Fast-forward to 2026 and several Pixel releases, and there's still nothing like it in the Android space. Samsung's Galaxy Ultra line, particularly the Galaxy S25 and S26 Ultra, has grown on me over the years, but as much as I like Samsung's smartphone hardware, I still find One UI a confusing mess of jumbled features, which is in stark contrast to the Pixel's stripped-down approach to Android.

Galaxy S26 Ultra review: Samsung's most complete phone yet

This is the first S series flagship that might pull me way from Google's Pixel line.

24
By  Patrick O'Rourke

The Pixel's take on Android can't be beat

There's an elegance in its iPhone-like simplicity

A key reason I'm locked into the Pixel world and always return to Google's devices after I finish reviewing whatever smartphone I'm reviewing, is its elegant operating system. It's simple, chilled-out, and easy to navigate, but it doesn't take itself too seriously and features fun animations and bright colors — Android 17's Material 3 Expressive just looks fantastic.

I'd even go so far as to say I prefer the Android version on my Pixel 10 Pro to iOS 18's Liquid Glass, which is more than a bit of a mess at times. Additionally, I know that as soon as the latest version of Android drops, I'll have access to it on my Pixel, since it's Google's flagship smartphone. While Samsung's One UI is often updated relatively quickly to the latest version of Android, it can sometimes take a few months for the latest version to arrive, and the same goes for other Android phones from companies like OnePlus, Nothing, and Motorola. With Pixel smartphones, I don't even need to think about getting the latest update — I know it'll be there on day one, including public and developer betas.

Google's smartphones feature Android's most balanced take on photography

The AI camera features are actually useful

Over the years, my smartphone has slowly become my go-to camera because it's the device I always have on me. The days of blown-out, grainy images have long since passed, as long as you're using a relatively recent mid-range to high-end device, whether it's Samsung's Galaxy S26, the iPhone 17, or a Pixel 10 Pro, leading to me leaving my DSLR at home far more often than I used to a few years ago.

I've always appreciated Google's focus on offering a more grounded take on photography with the Pixel line. The images are arguably truer to life than the iPhone's processing of photos. Colors might have a bit more vibrancy, but generally, every Pixel has shot great-looking images that don't look like they're from another planet, unlike Samsung's more out-there take on photography.

More recent Pixel smartphones, including the Pixel 9 Pro and Pixel 10 Pro, offer AI camera features that are useful and go well beyond adding stickers and random objects to photos. For example, Add Me lets you add yourself to a group shot by combining two separately shot images. The first time you use Add Me, it'll feel silly and like it won't work, but it's great and surprisingly useful. Another useful feature is Best Take, which analyzes similar images and merges them. For example, if you take a group shot and one person has their eyes closed, but everyone else looks great, but their eyes are open in the next image, Best Take combines both photos with the help of AI.

The Pixel has the sleekest design in the Android space

PixelSnap is a great addition, too

I know there are a lot of great-looking Android smartphones out there, and I've always appreciated Samsung's S series, but the simple elegance of the Pixel line just can't be beat, dating back to Google's first smartphone and its two-tone brushed aluminum-and-plastic back.

Subscribe to the newsletter for Pixel and Android insights

Joining our newsletter gives focused Pixel and Android coverage: practical camera tips, design breakdowns, and clear UI analysis to help you judge features and real-world value across modern smartphones.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

For example, the Pixel 10 Pro's matte rear is chilled-out and understated, while its oval camera island gives the device a unique, instantly recognizable look in an industry full of identical slab-like smartphones that all look the same (I felt the same way about iPhone 17 Pro redesign and its sizable camera bump).

It's a simple aesthetic that's basic yet fun, especially when you look at some of the more vibrant colors like the Pixel 10 Pro's Jade or my personal favorite, the Lemongrass Pixel 10. This isn't design-related, but I've also found that I really appreciate the Pixel 10 series' magnetic PixelSnap accessory ecosystem. MagSafe is one of the best things about the iPhone, and I continue to be baffled that other manufacturers haven't created their own magnetic accessory ecosystems now that Qi2 wireless charging is available (I'm looking at you, Samsung).

Pixel 10 Pro
$750 $1000 Save $250
Display
1280 x 2856 pixel resolution LTPO OLED at (495 PPI)
RAM
16GB
Storage
128GB
Rear camera
f/1.68 50-megapixel wide, f/1.7 48-megapixel ultrawide, f/2.8 28-megapixel telephoto
Front camera
f/2.2 42-megapixel
Battery
4,870 mAh
$750 at Google
6 features I immediately disable on my Google Pixel

Even Google's clean take on Android needs a bit of tweaking.

3
By  Patrick O'Rourke

The days of blown-out, grainy images have long since passed, as long as you're using a relatively recent mid-range to high-end device, whether it's Samsung's Galaxy S26, the iPhone 17, or a Pixel 10 Pro, leading to me leaving my DSLR at home far more often than I used to a few years ago.

I've always appreciated Google's focus on offering a more grounded take on photography with the Pixel line. The images are arguably truer to life than the iPhone's processing of photos. Colors might have a bit more vibrancy, but generally, every Pixel has shot great-looking images that don't look like they're from another planet, unlike Samsung's more out-there take on photography.

More recent Pixel smartphones, including the Pixel 9 Pro and Pixel 10 Pro, offer AI camera features that are useful and go well beyond adding stickers and random objects to photos. For example, Add Me lets you add yourself to a group shot by combining two separately shot images. The first time you use Add Me, it'll feel silly and like it won't work, but it's great and surprisingly useful. Another useful feature is Best Take, which analyzes similar images and merges them. For example, if you take a group shot and one person has their eyes closed, but everyone else looks great, but their eyes are open in the next image, Best Take combines both photos with the help of AI.

The Pixel has the sleekest design in the Android space

PixelSnap is a great addition, too

I know there are a lot of great-looking Android smartphones out there, and I've always appreciated Samsung's S series, but the simple elegance of the Pixel line just can't be beat, dating back to Google's first smartphone and its two-tone brushed aluminum-and-plastic back.

Subscribe to the newsletter for Pixel and Android insights

Joining our newsletter gives focused Pixel and Android coverage: practical camera tips, design breakdowns, and clear UI analysis to help you judge features and real-world value across modern smartphones.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

For example, the Pixel 10 Pro's matte rear is chilled-out and understated, while its oval camera island gives the device a unique, instantly recognizable look in an industry full of identical slab-like smartphones that all look the same (I felt the same way about iPhone 17 Pro redesign and its sizable camera bump).

It's a simple aesthetic that's basic yet fun, especially when you look at some of the more vibrant colors like the Pixel 10 Pro's Jade or my personal favorite, the Lemongrass Pixel 10. This isn't design-related, but I've also found that I really appreciate the Pixel 10 series' magnetic PixelSnap accessory ecosystem. MagSafe is one of the best things about the iPhone, and I continue to be baffled that other manufacturers haven't created their own magnetic accessory ecosystems now that Qi2 wireless charging is available (I'm looking at you, Samsung).

Pixel 10 Pro

1280 x 2856 pixel resolution LTPO OLED at (495 PPI)

16GB

f/1.68 50-megapixel wide, f/1.7 48-megapixel ultrawide, f/2.8 28-megapixel telephoto
Front camera
f/2.2 42-megapixel
Battery
4,870 mAh
$750 at Google
6 features I immediately disable on my Google Pixel

Even Google's clean take on Android needs a bit of tweaking.

3
By  Patrick O'Rourke
item.content:encoded:
When it comes to Android, I've been a Pixel user since the launch of Google's first Pixel smartphone in 2016.

It offers the cleanest take on Android, features excellent camera performance, and, more recently with the Pixel 9 and Pixel 10 series, actually useful AI features. It also remains one of the sleekest devices out there, especially after the recent switch from a visor-style rear camera bump that spans the width of the back of the phone to a cooler-looking oval island.

Fast-forward to 2026 and several Pixel releases, and there's still nothing like it in the Android space. Samsung's Galaxy Ultra line, particularly the Galaxy S25 and S26 Ultra, has grown on me over the years, but as much as I like Samsung's smartphone hardware, I still find One UI a confusing mess of jumbled features, which is in stark contrast to the Pixel's stripped-down approach to Android.

Galaxy S26 Ultra review: Samsung's most complete phone yet

This is the first S series flagship that might pull me way from Google's Pixel line.

24
By  Patrick O'Rourke

The Pixel's take on Android can't be beat

There's an elegance in its iPhone-like simplicity

A key reason I'm locked into the Pixel world and always return to Google's devices after I finish reviewing whatever smartphone I'm reviewing, is its elegant operating system. It's simple, chilled-out, and easy to navigate, but it doesn't take itself too seriously and features fun animations and bright colors — Android 17's Material 3 Expressive just looks fantastic.

I'd even go so far as to say I prefer the Android version on my Pixel 10 Pro to iOS 18's Liquid Glass, which is more than a bit of a mess at times. Additionally, I know that as soon as the latest version of Android drops, I'll have access to it on my Pixel, since it's Google's flagship smartphone. While Samsung's One UI is often updated relatively quickly to the latest version of Android, it can sometimes take a few months for the latest version to arrive, and the same goes for other Android phones from companies like OnePlus, Nothing, and Motorola. With Pixel smartphones, I don't even need to think about getting the latest update — I know it'll be there on day one, including public and developer betas.

Google's smartphones feature Android's most balanced take on photography

The AI camera features are actually useful

Over the years, my smartphone has slowly become my go-to camera because it's the device I always have on me. The days of blown-out, grainy images have long since passed, as long as you're using a relatively recent mid-range to high-end device, whether it's Samsung's Galaxy S26, the iPhone 17, or a Pixel 10 Pro, leading to me leaving my DSLR at home far more often than I used to a few years ago.

For example, if you take a group shot and one person has their eyes closed, but everyone else looks great, but their eyes are open in the next image, Best Take combines both photos with the help of AI.

PixelSnap is a great addition, too

I know there are a lot of great-looking Android smartphones out there, and I've always appreciated Samsung's S series, but the simple elegance of the Pixel line just can't be beat, dating back to Google's first smartphone and its two-tone brushed aluminum-and-plastic back.

item.title: Claude just turned Python from confusing into actually intuitive with one feature
item.itunes:title: Claude just turned Python from confusing into actually intuitive with one feature
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
I’m trying to learn Python, so I read as much as I can about it. But because I’m more of a visual learner, not everything I read sticks right away. That’s when I started seeing how useful Claude’s visual feature was for me. Instead of giving me another block of text to read, it could turn a Python concept into a visual right in the chat, making it easier to understand. What made it more helpful was that I could tell Claude to make it more interactive, add more details, or explain the idea as if I were a 12-year-old.

Claude made the Python concept easier to follow once I could interact with it

The visual roadmap helped me stop feeling lost, thanks to the buttons

The visual feature created three starting points:

  • Start here
  • Getting comfy
  • Ready to build

Each section had its own button that showed me more options. For example, when I clicked Start here, I saw sub-sections such as Foundations, Data structures, and Control flow. I took things one step at a time and clicked Variables & types, which was crossed out to show that I had already covered that section. This is helpful because it lets me keep track of what I’ve already read and stops me from wasting time rereading any info.

The visual feature was easy to navigate because the highlighted button showed me which section I was in. When I went back to a section to review, the button’s name was still crossed out, but only the last button I clicked on was highlighted in purple.

I paired Microsoft Excel with Claude, and it beats Copilot at its own game

Copilot who?

1
By  Mahnoor Faisal

I stopped reading about Python and started playing with it

The colors and live updates made the examples easier to follow

I started with the Start here section, followed by the Variables & types subsection, where I could see an example of how Python handles different number types. There was one red slider that I could use to see results in real time.

At first, I didn’t really get the difference between an int and a float. I just knew they were both numbers. But when I checked the Decimal box and watched the output switch from int to float, I finally understood it. Seeing that change made it much easier to understand how Python decides what type of number it’s working with. It turned into more research, but at least now I understand the topic better.

I could revisit any part of the visual as many times as I wanted

That kind of repetition made Python feel less overwhelming

Claude’s visual tool lets me go back to it whenever I need to, which is great because the more I review a topic, the more it sticks. The interactive visual didn’t give me any errors during my testing. I could go to the part I needed instead of starting from the beginning. I was also able to focus on the parts that were still a little confusing, rather than trying to build the same Python example in another app.

The tool worked great until I tried to go back to where I started

A few clicks were all it took to bury the original visual

At the bottom of the visual, I saw buttons like “Quiz me” that I could use to test my knowledge, but after a few clicks, the original visual got pushed so far up the conversation that I lost track of it. I kept clicking on more buttons, which led to another visual, then more questions, and then more research. By the time I wanted to review everything again, I tried using the scrollbar to jump back faster, but I kept overshooting it.

I still found a workaround when I wanted to revisit the visual

Turning it into an Artifact made it easier to open again later

To see the visual again, I turned it into an Artifact. Once I saved it as an Artifact, the Python visual opened in a side panel, making it much easier to find again. If I had other saved Artifacts, I could still spot it in the list and open it without having to dig through the conversation. Once I saved it, I felt such a sense of relief that I wouldn’t have to waste time finding it again.

Seeing what I was reading about made all the difference

When I see an example of what I’m learning, it makes sense faster. The buttons, sliders, and sections in Claude’s interactive visual gave me the help I needed to understand certain things about Python. I also liked how I could save it for later and review it as many times as I want. This is a tool I’m going to keep using for my other projects.

Claude
OS
Windows, macOS
Individual pricing
Free plan available; $17/month Pro plan

Claude is an AI assistant made by Anthropic that you can use for free to help you research, learn, and build things.

See at Official Site See at Claude

item.title: Voice assistants are getting worse at simple tasks, and nobody's talking about why
item.itunes:title: Voice assistants are getting worse at simple tasks, and nobody's talking about why
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
My smart home was designed to be voice-first, first with Google Assistant, then with Alexa, and a sprinkling of Echo Dots around the house. I was an early adopter of Alexa+, and the only feedback I could give about the change was "why?" From my extended usage, Alexa+ makes voice control shoddy at best, often falling over simple commands like turning on lights. That is, if she could find the lights, as I had to re-pair the entire house a couple of times when individual ones dropped off the radar.

The point is, the big names of voice assistants used to be better at their jobs. Well, except Siri. She's never been good at her job. But the rest of them all nosedived in accuracy in recent years, and you might already be guessing why.

My ESP32 voice assistant has a 3D printed home and GLaDOS's attitude

It looks good, sounds great, and it's pretty amusing.

4
By  Adam Conway

It's not just you, voice assistants are getting dumber

The least smart part of your smart home

The release of Alexa+ to all US users is just the latest domino to topple in the line of voice assistants falling over themselves. Google Assistant has been on virtual life support for the last couple of years. Siri might have been one of the first, but she's been a perennial also-ran since then, and Samsung fans hated Bixby so much that the company added a way to disable it on your phone.

Voice assistants have not been alright, not for a long time, and that's not okay. The devices that run these voice-activated assistants were sold on the premise that the voice part of the equation worked, and they just aren't in the sorry state they exist today.

Voice control seems to have a mind of its own

The good news is that you don't have to use those voice assistants anymore. Home Assistant has an open-source smart speaker that works without an internet connection, but it requires much more setup time than your average Alexa device. It's also not that much better than the big names, but you get privacy for your voice commands instead of sending transcripts to big tech, which is probably a good thing.

Home Assistant Voice Preview Edition
Dimensions
84x84x21 mm
Weight
96 g
See at Home Assistant

It matches up with Gen AI's rise

This is probably only a coincidence, right?

Voice assistants don't have to be everything at once; they're perfectly okay for those times when your hands are full, and you want to set a timer for the next stage of a recipe or turn the lights off as you're leaving a room. But they're not good at that anymore, often failing at simple tasks or jumping ahead to a solution before you've finished your command.

It's easiest to see why when you look at Google. Google Assistant is no more — it's been ousted by Google Gemini. Yes, generative AI is now in charge of your voice assistant, and Google is far from the only company that jumped on the AI train.

Alexa+ is also powered by AI, which is probably why she only turns on two of the three lights in my bedroom when I ask. And she's no longer good at setting timers or telling me the weather for the day ahead on the other side of the city, tasks that used to be effortless.

Sure, they can carry on a conversation a little longer, but that's not what anyone wanted. I can't be alone in not wanting a full-blown conversation about the thermostat settings, or if I turned off the lights on the first floor.

You're training your voice assistant now

When they first came on the scene, voice assistants were hard-coded into decision options. You might have one or two ways to ask for the lights to turn off, but either would achieve the same result without wavering. That meant you had to remember the commands, but it also meant lots of development time on the other side, because companies had to envision what you, as the user, would use as commands.

Subscribe for smarter takes on voice assistants

Get clarity on why voice assistants stumble and what that means for smart homes — subscribe to the newsletter for concise analysis of AI-driven voice features, privacy trade-offs, and practical guidance to help you navigate and troubleshoot smart-home voice tech.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Now, you're training the voice assistants, who have been foisted on your smart speakers like barely conscious toddlers. If a straightforward command doesn't get the lights to turn off, you could try a more rambling question, although Alexa+ has a habit of interrupting you mid-sentence if you say words like "time" or "weather" before continuing.

I added voice control to my home without Alexa, Google, or Apple, and it works better

Saying farewell to smart assistants.

By  Rich Edmonds

Here's to hoping they get smarter quickly

Voice assistants are no longer fully created by the product teams behind them. Instead, Gen AI is doing a lot of the heavy lifting, even for problems that were solved years ago. At some point they might catch up, and if they do, it won't be much longer until the voice assistants are much, much better but I'm not holding my breath.

item.content:encoded:
My smart home was designed to be voice-first, first with Google Assistant, then with Alexa, and a sprinkling of Echo Dots around the house. I was an early adopter of Alexa+, and the only feedback I could give about the change was "why?" From my extended usage, Alexa+ makes voice control shoddy at best, often falling over simple commands like turning on lights. That is, if she could find the lights, as I had to re-pair the entire house a couple of times when individual ones dropped off the radar.

The point is, the big names of voice assistants used to be better at their jobs. Well, except Siri. She's never been good at her job. But the rest of them all nosedived in accuracy in recent years, and you might already be guessing why.

My ESP32 voice assistant has a 3D printed home and GLaDOS's attitude

It looks good, sounds great, and it's pretty amusing.

4
By  Adam Conway

It's not just you, voice assistants are getting dumber

The least smart part of your smart home

The release of Alexa+ to all US users is just the latest domino to topple in the line of voice assistants falling over themselves. Google Assistant has been on virtual life support for the last couple of years. Siri might have been one of the first, but she's been a perennial also-ran since then, and Samsung fans hated Bixby so much that the company added a way to disable it on your phone.

Voice assistants have not been alright, not for a long time, and that's not okay. The devices that run these voice-activated assistants were sold on the premise that the voice part of the equation worked, and they just aren't in the sorry state they exist today.

Voice control seems to have a mind of its own

The good news is that you don't have to use those voice assistants anymore. Home Assistant has an open-source smart speaker that works without an internet connection, but it requires much more setup time than your average Alexa device. It's also not that much better than the big names, but you get privacy for your voice commands instead of sending transcripts to big tech, which is probably a good thing.

Home Assistant Voice Preview Edition
Dimensions
84x84x21 mm
Weight
96 g
See at Home Assistant

It matches up with Gen AI's rise

This is probably only a coincidence, right?

Voice assistants don't have to be everything at once; they're perfectly okay for those times when your hands are full, and you want to set a timer for the next stage of a recipe or turn the lights off as you're leaving a room. But they're not good at that anymore, often failing at simple tasks or jumping ahead to a solution before you've finished your command.

It's easiest to see why when you look at Google. Google Assistant is no more — it's been ousted by Google Gemini. Yes, generative AI is now in charge of your voice assistant, and Google is far from the only company that jumped on the AI train.

Alexa+ is also powered by AI, which is probably why she only turns on two of the three lights in my bedroom when I ask. And she's no longer good at setting timers or telling me the weather for the day ahead on the other side of the city, tasks that used to be effortless.

Sure, they can carry on a conversation a little longer, but that's not what anyone wanted. I can't be alone in not wanting a full-blown conversation about the thermostat settings, or if I turned off the lights on the first floor.

You're training your voice assistant now

When they first came on the scene, voice assistants were hard-coded into decision options. You might have one or two ways to ask for the lights to turn off, but either would achieve the same result without wavering. That meant you had to remember the commands, but it also meant lots of development time on the other side, because companies had to envision what you, as the user, would use as commands.

Subscribe for smarter takes on voice assistants

Get clarity on why voice assistants stumble and what that means for smart homes — subscribe to the newsletter for concise analysis of AI-driven voice features, privacy trade-offs, and practical guidance to help you navigate and troubleshoot smart-home voice tech.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Now, you're training the voice assistants, who have been foisted on your smart speakers like barely conscious toddlers. If a straightforward command doesn't get the lights to turn off, you could try a more rambling question, although Alexa+ has a habit of interrupting you mid-sentence if you say words like "time" or "weather" before continuing.

I added voice control to my home without Alexa, Google, or Apple, and it works better

Saying farewell to smart assistants.

By  Rich Edmonds

Here's to hoping they get smarter quickly

Voice assistants are no longer fully created by the product teams behind them. Instead, Gen AI is doing a lot of the heavy lifting, even for problems that were solved years ago. At some point they might catch up, and if they do, it won't be much longer until the voice assistants are much, much better but I'm not holding my breath.

item.title: I thought my Proxmox server was slow until I changed this setting
item.itunes:title: I thought my Proxmox server was slow until I changed this setting
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Considering the sheer number of settings, toggles, and menus in Proxmox’s web UI, you can spend hours tweaking the virtualization platform for your home labbing needs. There's an SDN stack for network lovers, high-availability services for hardcore self-hosting enthusiasts, and backup tools for folks who don’t want to lose their meticulously-crafted app stack to botched experiments.

Then you’ve got tools that dictate the behavior of virtual guests, and with Proxmox’s default settings prioritizing stability over all else, you might want to try tinkering with them for the best performance in your LXCs and VMs. Take the Display option hidden within your virtual machine settings, for example. While it’s a seemingly harmless setting, leaving it at the default value can cause Proxmox VMs to feel sluggish. Yeah, I speak from experience.

I tried using a Proxmox-based Windows 11 VM as my daily driver - here's how it went

All it took was a little bit of tinkering and a whole lot of patience

12
By  Ayush Pande

Switching to SPICE display made my Proxmox VMs a lot more responsive

I don’t need the SPICE client just to access their GUIs, either

During my early Proxmox days, I was always trying new things to enhance the performance of my virtual machines – partly because I wanted to create dev environments for my coding tasks, and also because the thought of turning virtualized Linux setups as responsive as my everyday system seemed equal parts impressive and unhinged. Allocating plenty of system resources and tweaking the CPU settings definitely improved the experience, but something was still amiss.

Moving the cursor was somewhat sluggish, no matter what OS I used for the VM. Likewise, launching anything on virtual machines with desktop environments would take slightly longer than it should. And these problems didn’t affect a specific system, either. Switching from my old PC to my Xeon server – or even arming my current gaming PC with Proxmox via a spare SSD – couldn’t fix the lag issues. Heck, even enabling the QEMU guest tools and passing a graphics card (one at a time, of course) to my virtual machines failed to make my everyday tasks feel more responsive.

That’s when I came across some forum posts claiming that the default Display option might be the culprit. And well, changing it to SPICE instantly made my virtual machines snappier. The cursor no longer left a trail when swiping across the screen, while switching between apps became a lot more seamless.

Your Proxmox 8 server stops getting security updates in August, and upgrading to PVE 9 isn't straightforward

Whatever you do, remember to back up your virtual guests

4
By  Ayush Pande

Here’s the fun part: if you try clicking on the Console button in the top right corner of the Proxmox web UI, it will download a .vv file on your client, and you’ll need to use it with a remote access app (something like Virt-viewer) to access the virtual machine’s GUI elements. However, you can sidestep this process entirely by using the Console tab on the left side of the screen, right where you’d find typical VM settings. While you probably won’t be able to use advanced SPICE features with this workaround, it’s my go-to method for tinkering with disposable virtual machines when I don’t want to configure access setups on my client devices.

It also fixed a weird issue with high-resolution desktop environments

Enhancing the snappiness of my virtual machines wasn’t the only advantage of enabling SPICE. When I used to change the display resolution on my Linux VMs (specifically instances relying on Debian-based distros) past 1440p, I’d start to encounter weird bugs. If the virtual machine crashed out of nowhere, I’d end up with a black screen after the initial login page – and this would only happen when I’d bump the resolution up a notch. Heck, I’d have VMs with this display issue even after a clean shutdown, and the only way to resolve it involved reloading to an old snapshot with the default 1024x576 resolution.

After I started switching my virtual machines to SPICE, this problem never reared its ugly head. It’s such a minor fix, but it solved a massive problem that troubled me countless times during my early Proxmox days.

But you might want to use different tools for remote gaming VMs

Parsec for a simple setup, Sunshine + Moonlight if you don’t mind tinkering a bit

I tend to switch the Display setting to SPICE on almost all Linux virtual machines, but my Windows 11 remote gaming and dev VMs are an exception. I opt for Windows 11 Pro for virtual machines, and it’s mostly so I can access them using RDP. It works exceedingly well for everyday tasks, even when I use the Windows App on my MacBook.

Subscribe to the newsletter for Proxmox tuning tips

Discover deeper Proxmox display and VM performance guides when you subscribe to the newsletter. Expect practical tweaks, troubleshooting walkthroughs, and measured comparisons of SPICE, RDP, Parsec, Sunshine/Moonlight to help optimize your setups.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

However, both SPICE and RDP are no match for remote game-streaming services and their extremely low latency, which is why I rely on the latter for wacky VM setups that can harness my GPUs to run modern titles. Personally, I’m not a fan of Parsec’s paywalled features or its tendency to use company servers to authenticate my devices. But it’s by far the simplest method for playing games remotely on a virtual machine, especially since it bundles a virtual display adapter with the app.

Otherwise, if you’ve got a dummy HDMI display, or you're willing to jump into the labyrinth of virtual displays, a Sunshine + Moonlight (or Apollo + Artemis, for Android clients) setup delivers a better experience without relying on external servers.

Proxmox
See at Proxmox

item.title: Rainmeter turned my boring Windows desktop into something I actually enjoy looking at every day
item.itunes:title: Rainmeter turned my boring Windows desktop into something I actually enjoy looking at every day
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Windows customization has been a thing ever since I bought my first laptop. I installed Windows 7 on it, and if you are from that era, you can understand that the OS's appearance wasn't stellar. 14 years later, Windows 11 looks much better than its predecessors, but not to the extent I want. For the sake of customization, I can change the background and accent color, and that's it. Microsoft scrapped individual widgets and brought the widgets board, which is just a fancy pop-up dashboard. Even the default themes are bland, which prompted me to bring back the trusty old rain meter.

Most Windows themes are just glorified wallpaper packs that don't do anything beyond that. Rainmeter is the exact opposite, as it offers endless customization options. It's so deep and granular that you might be overwhelmed with the choice in front of you. Let's discuss my experience of using Rainmeter to build a custom desktop.

Picking Rainmeter over theme packs

It's on a different level

Customizing the desktop is more than wallpaper carousels and accent colors. A traditional theme available on the Microsoft Store looks great, but it only tweaks these two elements and leaves out everything else. What if I want to change the included elements? What about adding widgets that display the system information or pull data from the web?

Windows widgets are already in a sad state, and Rainmeter can solve all of these problems. Rainmeter uses a different concept of skins that contain all these elements (some might focus on a select few), with more customization than you get with any other tool.

The initial setup of Rainmeter isn't as troubling as you might expect. It's a straightforward install, and you don't need to do anything special, disable security features, or change system policies to get it working. I also haven't encountered any problems with Windows updates. I previously used Explorer Patcher, but it stopped working after a Windows update. It took quite a while before developers could get it back working.

5 apps that give you the desktop widgets Microsoft won’t

Ditch Windows’ boring widgets! Get real customization with these tools and make your desktop truly yours

2
By  Pranav Bhardwaj

Building the best version of my desktop

Abundant options

Rainmeter isn't like any other theme pack or customization utility with a limited set of options. You'll easily find multiple website hosting skins that you can directly install and apply to your PC. This abundance of options is a bit confusing, as you end up scrolling through a lot of options to find the “best” thing.

After installation, Rainmeter’s default skin was pretty much sufficient for viewing most of the data, which included system stats, weather, disks, and more. However, the default skin looks severely outdated, and I couldn’t stick with it for long.

The best thing about Rainmeter is its ability to let you use multiple skins at once. This feature is extremely important because you can pick any element from any skin and then build a beautiful desktop layout with it. Unlike a theme, you can mix and match desktop elements (widgets) and place them carefully across the screen.

For my setup, I used elements from three skins. The first one is Irene, which was an impulsive download, but I didn’t like all the elements. The Recycle Bin and Date widget stood out the most, so I used both of them on one side of the desktop.

Next, I needed some hardware stats, so I added the SysDash skin, which has every possible resource stats widget you might need. It has CPU, GPU, network, drives, and more options, but I used the network, temperature, and CPU widgets. The weather widget was also cool, so I placed it next to Irene's date widget.

My right-hand side of the desktop was ready at this point with all the data I needed, but the left felt pretty empty. To fill it, I stumbled upon another stat-driven skin named Syntage. It was almost similar to SysDash, but had a vertical dock with it. However, it’s not the macOS dock that every skin tries to include. Syntage populates the social websites and a few Windows directories.

I can directly access my social sites, Reddit, browser, and File Explorer with it. The dock eliminates my need for the Taskbar, so I hid it using the Taskbar settings page. I also added a music playback widget below the dock to fill the empty space. The last piece of the puzzle was a good, dark wallpaper, and I used the Microsoft Store for it. It’s the wallpaper from “Dark and Light theme by Nick Boyer” and looks great on my system.

Since all the widgets are light-colored, the stark, dark background makes them pop out. Likewise, you must also pick a wallpaper that complements the widget's color. Don’t mix light and dark-colored widgets; otherwise, they’ll be illegible.

Control and customizations

Rainmeter doesn’t hold back

Adding elements to the desktop is merely the starting point. Rainmeter lets you individually tweak the position, transparency, hover actions, loading order, and more for each skin element. Honestly speaking, the drag-and-drop support works fine, and you can reposition elements without going into the settings. The transparency effect is useful if you want to blend the appearance with the background.

Level up your Windows desktop and more — subscribe to the newsletter

Subscribing to the newsletter gives curated Rainmeter skin picks, hands-on customization walkthroughs, and practical layout ideas you can copy. It also covers configuration tips, community favorites, and broader PC customization topics to refine your Windows desktop.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Rainmeter has a dedicated game mode that unloads all skin layouts when you open a game or a demanding app. There are several other settings that you can fiddle with, but I mostly keep using these, and the system tray icon to load or unload some skin elements. My current layout doesn’t devour memory and stays under the 100 MB mark most of the time. It’s a nice thing because I don’t want to sacrifice performance to keep my desktop appealing.

Legacy customization tool

Rainmeter is still relevant today due to its huge community, and finding new skins is never a problem. There are paid skins available on some websites, but if you are into custom layouts, you can mix and match several elements as I did. Rainmeter is completely free, and the level of control you get with it is difficult to find elsewhere.

Rainmeter

Rainmeter is a piece of software for Windows that you can use to customize your desktop. There are a ton of skins and plugins to make your PC look exactly how you want it!

See at Rainmeter

I also added a music playback widget below the dock to fill the empty space. The last piece of the puzzle was a good, dark wallpaper, and I used the Microsoft Store for it. It’s the wallpaper from “Dark and Light theme by Nick Boyer” and looks great on my system.

Since all the widgets are light-colored, the stark, dark background makes them pop out. Likewise, you must also pick a wallpaper that complements the widget's color. Don’t mix light and dark-colored widgets; otherwise, they’ll be illegible.

Control and customizations

Rainmeter doesn’t hold back

Adding elements to the desktop is merely the starting point. Rainmeter lets you individually tweak the position, transparency, hover actions, loading order, and more for each skin element. Honestly speaking, the drag-and-drop support works fine, and you can reposition elements without going into the settings. The transparency effect is useful if you want to blend the appearance with the background.

Level up your Windows desktop and more — subscribe to the newsletter

Subscribing to the newsletter gives curated Rainmeter skin picks, hands-on customization walkthroughs, and practical layout ideas you can copy. It also covers configuration tips, community favorites, and broader PC customization topics to refine your Windows desktop.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Rainmeter has a dedicated game mode that unloads all skin layouts when you open a game or a demanding app. There are several other settings that you can fiddle with, but I mostly keep using these, and the system tray icon to load or unload some skin elements. My current layout doesn’t devour memory and stays under the 100 MB mark most of the time. It’s a nice thing because I don’t want to sacrifice performance to keep my desktop appealing.

Legacy customization tool

Rainmeter is still relevant today due to its huge community, and finding new skins is never a problem. There are paid skins available on some websites, but if you are into custom layouts, you can mix and match several elements as I did. Rainmeter is completely free, and the level of control you get with it is difficult to find elsewhere.

Rainmeter

Rainmeter is a piece of software for Windows that you can use to customize your desktop. There are a ton of skins and plugins to make your PC look exactly how you want it!

See at Rainmeter
item.content:encoded:
Windows customization has been a thing ever since I bought my first laptop. I installed Windows 7 on it, and if you are from that era, you can understand that the OS's appearance wasn't stellar. 14 years later, Windows 11 looks much better than its predecessors, but not to the extent I want. For the sake of customization, I can change the background and accent color, and that's it. Microsoft scrapped individual widgets and brought the widgets board, which is just a fancy pop-up dashboard. Even the default themes are bland, which prompted me to bring back the trusty old rain meter.

Most Windows themes are just glorified wallpaper packs that don't do anything beyond that. Rainmeter is the exact opposite, as it offers endless customization options. It's so deep and granular that you might be overwhelmed with the choice in front of you. Let's discuss my experience of using Rainmeter to build a custom desktop.

Picking Rainmeter over theme packs

It's on a different level

Customizing the desktop is more than wallpaper carousels and accent colors. A traditional theme available on the Microsoft Store looks great, but it only tweaks these two elements and leaves out everything else. What if I want to change the included elements? What about adding widgets that display the system information or pull data from the web?

Windows widgets are already in a sad state, and Rainmeter can solve all of these problems. Rainmeter uses a different concept of skins that contain all these elements (some might focus on a select few), with more customization than you get with any other tool.

The initial setup of Rainmeter isn't as troubling as you might expect. It's a straightforward install, and you don't need to do anything special, disable security features, or change system policies to get it working. I also haven't encountered any problems with Windows updates. I previously used Explorer Patcher, but it stopped working after a Windows update. It took quite a while before developers could get it back working.

5 apps that give you the desktop widgets Microsoft won’t

Ditch Windows’ boring widgets! Get real customization with these tools and make your desktop truly yours

2
By  Pranav Bhardwaj

Building the best version of my desktop

Abundant options

Rainmeter isn't like any other theme pack or customization utility with a limited set of options. You'll easily find multiple website hosting skins that you can directly install and apply to your PC. This abundance of options is a bit confusing, as you end up scrolling through a lot of options to find the “best” thing.

After installation, Rainmeter’s default skin was pretty much sufficient for viewing most of the data, which included system stats, weather, disks, and more. However, the default skin looks severely outdated, and I couldn’t stick with it for long.

The best thing about Rainmeter is its ability to let you use multiple skins at once. This feature is extremely important because you can pick any element from any skin and then build a beautiful desktop layout with it. Unlike a theme, you can mix and match desktop elements (widgets) and place them carefully across the screen.

For my setup, I used elements from three skins. The first one is Irene, which was an impulsive download, but I didn’t like all the elements. The Recycle Bin and Date widget stood out the most, so I used both of them on one side of the desktop.

Next, I needed some hardware stats, so I added the SysDash skin, which has every possible resource stats widget you might need. It has CPU, GPU, network, drives, and more options, but I used the network, temperature, and CPU widgets. The weather widget was also cool, so I placed it next to Irene's date widget.

My right-hand side of the desktop was ready at this point with all the data I needed, but the left felt pretty empty. To fill it, I stumbled upon another stat-driven skin named Syntage. It was almost similar to SysDash, but had a vertical dock with it. However, it’s not the macOS dock that every skin tries to include. Syntage populates the social websites and a few Windows directories.

I can directly access my social sites, Reddit, browser, and File Explorer with it. The dock eliminates my need for the Taskbar, so I hid it using the Taskbar settings page. I also added a music playback widget below the dock to fill the empty space. The last piece of the puzzle was a good, dark wallpaper, and I used the Microsoft Store for it. It’s the wallpaper from “Dark and Light theme by Nick Boyer” and looks great on my system.

Since all the widgets are light-colored, the stark, dark background makes them pop out. Likewise, you must also pick a wallpaper that complements the widget's color. Don’t mix light and dark-colored widgets; otherwise, they’ll be illegible.

Control and customizations

Rainmeter doesn’t hold back

Adding elements to the desktop is merely the starting point. Rainmeter lets you individually tweak the position, transparency, hover actions, loading order, and more for each skin element. Honestly speaking, the drag-and-drop support works fine, and you can reposition elements without going into the settings. The transparency effect is useful if you want to blend the appearance with the background.

Level up your Windows desktop and more — subscribe to the newsletter

Subscribing to the newsletter gives curated Rainmeter skin picks, hands-on customization walkthroughs, and practical layout ideas you can copy. It also covers configuration tips, community favorites, and broader PC customization topics to refine your Windows desktop.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Rainmeter has a dedicated game mode that unloads all skin layouts when you open a game or a demanding app. There are several other settings that you can fiddle with, but I mostly keep using these, and the system tray icon to load or unload some skin elements. My current layout doesn’t devour memory and stays under the 100 MB mark most of the time. It’s a nice thing because I don’t want to sacrifice performance to keep my desktop appealing.

Legacy customization tool

Rainmeter is still relevant today due to its huge community, and finding new skins is never a problem. There are paid skins available on some websites, but if you are into custom layouts, you can mix and match several elements as I did. Rainmeter is completely free, and the level of control you get with it is difficult to find elsewhere.

Rainmeter

Rainmeter is a piece of software for Windows that you can use to customize your desktop. There are a ton of skins and plugins to make your PC look exactly how you want it!

See at Rainmeter

item.title: Your motherboard has a 1GbE port, and adding a $15 2.5GbE PCIe card changes everything about your network
item.itunes:title: Your motherboard has a 1GbE port, and adding a $15 2.5GbE PCIe card changes everything about your network
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
You pay for 2Gbps fiber internet, but your PC somehow still only downloads at around 940 Mbps. That's because your motherboard's 1GbE port is a hard physical limit — 1,000 Mbps is its maximum, and after overhead, you'll never see more than about 115MBps of real-world transfer speed.

After network overhead, you'll never see more than 115 Mbps of real-world transfer speed.

For less than the price of a mouse pad, a 2.5GbE PCIe card can more than double your bandwidth using the cables you already have in your walls. The 1GbE port is the 1080p of networking. It's universal and reliable, but it is becoming increasingly a bottleneck. Whilst most motherboards from the last five years still ship with a standard 1GbE PCIe, our home data needs, which are driven by Wi-Fi 7 multi-gigabit fiber internet and massive 4K video libraries, have simply outgrown it. Adding a cheap 2.5GbE PCIe card is the single most cost-effective hardware upgrade you can make to your PC this year.

TP-Link TX201 2.5 Gigabit PCI Express Ethernet Network Adapter

Picking up the TP-Link TX201 2.5 Gigabit PCI Express Ethernet Network Adapter will allow you to take advantage of your full fiber speeds.

See at Amazon

One of the easiest and cheapest upgrades

With a difference you'll actually feel

While upgrading to a 2.5GbE PCIe card might feel a little intimidating, it really is plug-and-play. Options like the TP-Link TX201 2.5 Gigabit PCI Express Ethernet Network Adapter are the most popular thanks to their being so budget-friendly. It's one PCIe slot, and for the most part, it's the one that people mostly leave empty and ignored under their GPU. You can install it the exact same way you'd install a PCIe SSD or other similar peripheral.

2.5GbE is the absolute sweet spot. Whilst you're looking at potential upgrades for your PC when it comes to networking, you might think of opting for 10GbE, which is faster. Being honest, it's a vanity upgrade for most. 10GbE cards are expensive, run hot, and usually require you to rip out your old Cat5e cables and replace them with Cat6a. 2.5GbE is designed to run perfectly over standard Cat5e cabling up to 30m and up to 100m over Cat6, meaning it is simply a plug-and-play upgrade that doesn't require a whole-home renovation.

If you own a network-attached storage (NAS) or even a home server, 1GbE is a massive bottleneck for all of your backups. Moving a 100GB 4K video project from your PC to your NAS takes about 13–14 minutes on a 1GbE connection, but drops to about seven minutes on a 2GbE connection, and under five minutes on a 2.5GbE connection. You can edit video or browse massive photo libraries directly from the server without thumbnail lag or buffering issues.

Multi-gigabit routers are pointless without this one upgrade

Take full advantage of your speeds with this addition

16
By  Jasmine Mannan

You'll also come across internet service providers now offering 1.2Gbps, 1.5Gbps, or even higher fiber plans. However, if you plug a 1GbE port into a 2Gbps line, you are literally throwing half of the service away that you pay for every single month. If you're paying for a high fiber plan and don't have a 2.5GbE port, you aren't even physically able to take advantage of this fiber speed on your PC.

There are other perks, too

And also downsides to consider

There are also other hidden benefits to upgrading. It's not just about top speed; it's about headroom when your line is pegged at 95% capacity on your 1GbE. Your latency will spike; however, 2.5GbE provides breathing room, so your Discord call doesn't lag when you're moving files in the background, too. It's also a great way to future-proof your device, as Wi-Fi 6E and 7 are becoming standard over the next few years. Your wireless devices will soon be faster than your wired PC unless you upgrade the port.

However, it's worth managing your expectations. A 2.5GbE card in your PC won't do much if it's plugged into a 1GbE router. You will need a 2.5GbE switch if you don't already have one; however, these are relatively cheap and can be picked up for $30-$50 to bridge the gap. Also, keep in mind the storage bottleneck. If you're transferring to an old mechanical HDD, then 2.5GbE won't really help. This upgrade is specifically for the SSD era.

If you're yet to swap, now is the time

No need to rewire your entire home

Switching to a 2.5 GbE card is the lowest-cost upgrade with the greatest tangible impact for the majority of power users. If you're upgrading your router, considering swapping to Cat6 cabling, and just generally concerned about your network speeds, but you're yet to upgrade to a 2.5 GbE PCIe card, then now might be the time to make the swap. Stop letting a 20-year-old networking standard gatekeep your modern hardware and realize the fiber speeds that you're paying for.

Subscribe to the newsletter for 2.5GbE upgrade advice

Get actionable coverage by subscribing to the newsletter: clear guidance on upgrading to 2.5GbE, practical product picks, installation steps using existing Cat5e wiring, and how to avoid common pitfalls so you can actually use the fiber speeds you pay for.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

item.title: I switched from ChatGPT Plus to Claude Pro, and I haven’t regretted my decision
item.itunes:title: I switched from ChatGPT Plus to Claude Pro, and I haven’t regretted my decision
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
When choosing which LLM to use, it was quite simple at the beginning. ChatGPT was the clear and obvious front-runner for general use when I began using one daily, so when it became time to purchase a subscription, ChatGPT Plus was what I went with. It had my conversation history, non-identifying logs from my home lab, and I was familiar with how to prompt it to get what I wanted. I would sometimes put the same prompt into Anthropic's Claude, just to see what it would say, but besides that, my AI usage has been largely limited to local LLMs and ChatGPT. That is, until now.

Anthropic has been pretty difficult to ignore of late. In just the month of March alone, they added memory capability to the free plan, released support for Excel and PowerPoint, added the ability to assign tasks to your computer from your phone, and added the ability for Claude Code to open your apps, click through UI, and test builds on its own. That's really just scratching the surface. I'm not a developer, and though I've historically used the LLM component of AI primarily, switching to Claude Pro has really opened my eyes to what this technology is actually capable of beyond that.

I cancelled my ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Gemini subscriptions for Claude — and I should have sooner

Wish I did this sooner.

51
By  Mahnoor Faisal

The surrounding experience is much richer than ChatGPT

It feels a lot less scattered

From the moment I subscribed, I could tell that Claude was way less "LLM" and much more "collection of tools with an LLM on the side." Sonnet 4.6 is generally fine for the things I used it for as a free user, and compared to GPT 5.4, there isn't much to separate them in day-to-day usage. I gave it a simple task of searching for gym membership prices in my area—a task that I've asked of ChatGPT before, and it did quite well, providing a nice, clean table for me to quickly glance at. So far, so good.

The stuff around the chat function is where Claude is really able to show off. Cowork is an excellent example of that. After connecting it with Slack, Google, and my calendar, it's able to catch me up on everything I missed with a single click. These apps all have their own version of an "AI overview" that does a similar thing, but instead of going and looking at each thing individually, I can click one button and then go make my morning coffee, and by the time I come back, Claude has shown me everything I've missed. It can perform much more complex tasks, like analyzing data for you or organizing files on your system, depending on how much you trust it, of course.

I tested Claude’s new interactive visuals, and they’re changing how I explain things

Most LLMs suffer with visualisation, Claude doesn't

2
By  Abhinav Raj

Claude Code was easier to vibe-code with as a novice developer

ChatGPT's coding capability feels tacked on

Before I speak about anything relating to programming, it must be known that my coding ability is quite rudimentary. I learned HTML, CSS, and Python in school, so I have a basic understanding of the principles, but I am by no means an expert. This might land me straight in the "danger zone" in terms of software development, in other words, I know just enough to get myself into trouble.

I previously attempted to vibe-code a Python command-line script that would take CapFrameX .csv files as input and output a nice looking benchmark graph—a simple task that it should be able to handle. ChatGPT was fairly helpful in getting my initial scribblings into a somewhat functional script, but I had trouble with getting arguments to be properly passed to the functions. It also seemed to have a tendency to take the path of least resistance; if I asked it to add something to the script, it would quite literally tack it on instead of making a change that a real developer would.

I started a Claude Code session in VS Code and asked it to review the script for errors and any opportunities to simplify. I added that the command-line arguments are difficult to use, and it dropped arguments relating to per-run frametime outputs and distribution graphs, and instead opted to always generate both when invoking the script. This was a useful change, and the newly generated script with the changes worked on the first try. I then asked it to modify the script to create a folder with each new run, and modify the colors of the chart output to be in XDA's color scheme. The result was clean looking performance charts that are almost publication ready.

I use ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity, and Gemini daily — here's the only one worth paying for

One stands above the rest.

16
By  Mahnoor Faisal

The usage limits are difficult to ignore

I hit them extremely fast

These extra bells and whistles do come at a steep usage cost, and Anthropic's usage limits are extremely thin, even for Pro subcribers. Within an hour of testing a couple of Cowork capabilities and fixing my chart-generating script, I had hit 70% usage. One more Cowork task and I ended up at 100% for my current session, which felt a bit insane. For the $31 CAD I paid for one month, running into usage limits within an hour felt kind of ridiculous, whereas when I used ChatGPT Plus, I never ran into any limits, and it was also cheaper, coming in at just $25 CAD a month. OpenAI doesn't specify specific usage limits with Plus, just the ability to "have long chats over multiple sessions."

I use ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity, and Gemini daily — here's the only one worth paying for

One stands above the rest.

16
By  Mahnoor Faisal

I still don't regret swapping over

Despite the higher price and strict usage limits, Claude Pro felt much more integrated with the workflows I trusted it with, while ChatGPT felt like it was a tool I went to when I needed an LLM. I don't regret my decision to switch over, at least in the interim, as more of my LLM usage can be seemingly relegated to a free plan. The agentic tools that Claude has, along with its coding prowess, proved to me that it's worth toughing out the limits, at least until something else catches up.

In just the month of March alone, they added memory capability to the free plan, released support for Excel and PowerPoint, added the ability to assign tasks to your computer from your phone, and added the ability for Claude Code to open your apps, click through UI, and test builds on its own. That's really just scratching the surface. I'm not a developer, and though I've historically used the LLM component of AI primarily, switching to Claude Pro has really opened my eyes to what this technology is actually capable of beyond that.

I cancelled my ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Gemini subscriptions for Claude — and I should have sooner

Wish I did this sooner.

51
By  Mahnoor Faisal

The surrounding experience is much richer than ChatGPT

It feels a lot less scattered

From the moment I subscribed, I could tell that Claude was way less "LLM" and much more "collection of tools with an LLM on the side." Sonnet 4.6 is generally fine for the things I used it for as a free user, and compared to GPT 5.4, there isn't much to separate them in day-to-day usage. I gave it a simple task of searching for gym membership prices in my area—a task that I've asked of ChatGPT before, and it did quite well, providing a nice, clean table for me to quickly glance at. So far, so good.

The stuff around the chat function is where Claude is really able to show off. Cowork is an excellent example of that. After connecting it with Slack, Google, and my calendar, it's able to catch me up on everything I missed with a single click. These apps all have their own version of an "AI overview" that does a similar thing, but instead of going and looking at each thing individually, I can click one button and then go make my morning coffee, and by the time I come back, Claude has shown me everything I've missed. It can perform much more complex tasks, like analyzing data for you or organizing files on your system, depending on how much you trust it, of course.

I tested Claude’s new interactive visuals, and they’re changing how I explain things

Most LLMs suffer with visualisation, Claude doesn't

2
By  Abhinav Raj

Claude Code was easier to vibe-code with as a novice developer

ChatGPT's coding capability feels tacked on

Before I speak about anything relating to programming, it must be known that my coding ability is quite rudimentary. I learned HTML, CSS, and Python in school, so I have a basic understanding of the principles, but I am by no means an expert. This might land me straight in the "danger zone" in terms of software development, in other words, I know just enough to get myself into trouble.

I previously attempted to vibe-code a Python command-line script that would take CapFrameX .csv files as input and output a nice looking benchmark graph—a simple task that it should be able to handle. ChatGPT was fairly helpful in getting my initial scribblings into a somewhat functional script, but I had trouble with getting arguments to be properly passed to the functions. It also seemed to have a tendency to take the path of least resistance; if I asked it to add something to the script, it would quite literally tack it on instead of making a change that a real developer would.

I started a Claude Code session in VS Code and asked it to review the script for errors and any opportunities to simplify. I added that the command-line arguments are difficult to use, and it dropped arguments relating to per-run frametime outputs and distribution graphs, and instead opted to always generate both when invoking the script. This was a useful change, and the newly generated script with the changes worked on the first try. I then asked it to modify the script to create a folder with each new run, and modify the colors of the chart output to be in XDA's color scheme. The result was clean looking performance charts that are almost publication ready.

I use ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity, and Gemini daily — here's the only one worth paying for

One stands above the rest.

16
By  Mahnoor Faisal

The usage limits are difficult to ignore

I hit them extremely fast

These extra bells and whistles do come at a steep usage cost, and Anthropic's usage limits are extremely thin, even for Pro subcribers. Within an hour of testing a couple of Cowork capabilities and fixing my chart-generating script, I had hit 70% usage. One more Cowork task and I ended up at 100% for my current session, which felt a bit insane. For the $31 CAD I paid for one month, running into usage limits within an hour felt kind of ridiculous, whereas when I used ChatGPT Plus, I never ran into any limits, and it was also cheaper, coming in at just $25 CAD a month. OpenAI doesn't specify specific usage limits with Plus, just the ability to "have long chats over multiple sessions."

I use ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity, and Gemini daily — here's the only one worth paying for

One stands above the rest.

16
By  Mahnoor Faisal

I still don't regret swapping over

Despite the higher price and strict usage limits, Claude Pro felt much more integrated with the workflows I trusted it with, while ChatGPT felt like it was a tool I went to when I needed an LLM. I don't regret my decision to switch over, at least in the interim, as more of my LLM usage can be seemingly relegated to a free plan. The agentic tools that Claude has, along with its coding prowess, proved to me that it's worth toughing out the limits, at least until something else catches up.
item.content:encoded:
When choosing which LLM to use, it was quite simple at the beginning. ChatGPT was the clear and obvious front-runner for general use when I began using one daily, so when it became time to purchase a subscription, ChatGPT Plus was what I went with. It had my conversation history, non-identifying logs from my home lab, and I was familiar with how to prompt it to get what I wanted. I would sometimes put the same prompt into Anthropic's Claude, just to see what it would say, but besides that, my AI usage has been largely limited to local LLMs and ChatGPT. That is, until now.

Anthropic has been pretty difficult to ignore of late. In just the month of March alone, they added memory capability to the free plan, released support for Excel and PowerPoint, added the ability to assign tasks to your computer from your phone, and added the ability for Claude Code to open your apps, click through UI, and test builds on its own. That's really just scratching the surface. I'm not a developer, and though I've historically used the LLM component of AI primarily, switching to Claude Pro has really opened my eyes to what this technology is actually capable of beyond that.

I cancelled my ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Gemini subscriptions for Claude — and I should have sooner

Wish I did this sooner.
