item.title: I'm making these 4 PC tweaks non-negotiable before summer heat arrives
item.itunes:title: I'm making these 4 PC tweaks non-negotiable before summer heat arrives
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
It only took a cursory glance at my AIO's display last week to realize that summer was truly on its way. Seeing my idle temperatures jump from a steady 35°C to 50°C within the span of a couple of days was not a pleasant sight. As ambient room temperatures continue to climb, your PC's cooling capacity naturally becomes insufficient, causing fans to run louder and pushing your components towards their thermal ceiling much faster than they would in the winter.

What most users don't realize, however, is that you don't really have to trade your PC's peak performance just because the weather changed. Since I don't wish to settle for louder acoustics, here are the four most trusted, non-negotiable tweaks I'm relying on to keep the rig's temperatures low and performance intact.

Undervolting the CPU

For lower temps and better performance

To many enthusiasts, the idea that providing a CPU with less power can yield better performance sounds like a rather strange paradox. And why wouldn't it? Hardware enthusiasts are conditioned to expect constant trade-offs, where more speed usually necessitates more heat, and the whole proposition of undervolting feels like getting something for nothing. This really isn't witchcraft, but rather, a consequence of manufacturers not optimizing the voltage-frequency curve for your particular chip.

I've found that applying a -15 offset via the Ryzen Master utility's Curve Optimized produces the most immediate effects, especially under load. Instead of the temperature climbing towards the usual 81°C while running Fortnite, the chip comfortably hovers around 74-78°C mark. This isn't a performance compromise, either, because its performance in Cinebench tests almost always beats stock scores.

It's important to test the tolerance of your specific chip when it comes to undervolting. It is recommended to use the curve optimizer in multiples of 5 and properly test stability thereafter.

A thorough clean-up every now and then

Compressed air does the trick

Perhaps one of the most impactful performance uplift you can get is by simply removing the dust buildup from your rig. Dust can act as an insulating blanket, and I am always surprised by the amount of dirt hidden within the AIO radiator fins. If you're using an air cooler, it's also worth looking into the heat sink for any persistent build-up. Since I've made it a point to clean the tower at least once a month, I've invested in a handy compressed air blower for $20.

Clearing this build-up can potentially restore the equilibrium between positive and negative airflow in your rig, and ensure that heat exits the chassis instead of saturating your components. On the plus side, it also makes your PC look great.

If you're using power tools such as an electric compressed air blower, it's important to hold the fan fins steady to not damage the fan bearings unintentionally.

Reducing the GPU's power limit

Your GPU can do more with less

Undervolting a GPU requires some degree of precision and tweaks to the V/F curve until optimum performance and efficiency are achieved, but adjusting your GPU's power limit is a little more straightforward than that. It's based on the same principle, but with a key difference, which has everything to do with the power ceiling.

When you're undervolting, your card with the stock power limit can still pull peak wattage if the workload is demanding enough. By contrast, reducing the power limit directly caps energy consumption, which results in a more significant reduction in the heat output than undervolting alone.

Power limiting and undervolting can be used in combination for greater efficiency for your GPU, but doing so requires testing for stability by gradually adjusting the V/F curve.

Turning off Precision Boost Overdrive or Multicore Enhancement

You can always turn it back on when you really need it

When temps are high enough for me to reach for the system BIOS, the first thing I reach for is disabling the "auto-overclocking" features like AMD's Precision Boost Overdrive (PBO) or Intel's Multicore Enhancement (MCE). These settings are designed to push one or more CPU cores by instructing the chip to go beyond factory power limits, and as a result, impose an unwanted thermal tax. Sometimes, MCE and PBO are enabled by default in BIOS by manufacturers.

I've found that these profiles are not strictly necessary for all use cases. For light workloads or casual gaming, one does not need motherboard-applied overclocking, and there are effectively zero noticeable downsides to disabling them if you're already using a powerful processor.

5 hidden BIOS tweaks to slash your power usage and temperatures

Achieve a cooler system without losing performance

4
By  Tanveer Singh

Your PC can adapt to the weather just as well as you can

Credit: 

The PC is highly versatile and modular, and that isn't news at all. None of the tweaks I've mentioned so far demand a hardware upgrade or a deep understanding of your PC, which is what makes them accessible and practical even for most users. Whether you choose to apply them altogether, or just the ones most suited for your needs, you can optimize your PC's thermals for any weather.

item.title: I tried to customize VLC with its official skin editor, and I learned why nobody uses it
item.itunes:title: I tried to customize VLC with its official skin editor, and I learned why nobody uses it
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
VLC is one of the most respected and well-known media players in the world, thanks to its wide range of supported formats and great performance. One thing you may not realize is that VLC actually supports skins, though only through a separate executable that can actually load skins, which looks quite different from the typical VLC interface.

And what's cool about this is that not only are there plenty of skins on VLC's website to choose from, but there's also a skin editor that's supposed to make it easier to create your own skins for VLC. I wanted to try it out, and while I like the idea, you might need a lot of patience to go through with it yourself.

VLC's skin editor is very old

It's been almost 17 years

I went into this hoping for a fun and easy way to customize my VLC experience, but things are already not very promising when you look at the release date for the latest version of the VLC skin editor. The latest version is 0.8.5, which already suggests it's somewhat unfinished, but what's really concerning is that this version was released back in 2009.

Yes, this piece of software hasn't been updated in about 17 years, and if that's not enough context, here's another way to look at it: VLC version 1.0, the first properly stable release, came out in 2010. This version of the skin editor is older than even the first official release of VLC, and there have been dozens of updates since, including two major version jumps.

It's sort of a miracle that the skin editor is still usable at all, but even then, using it will require a little bit of research. The website says you need the Java Runtime Environment version 6 or higher, but if you install the latest version available, it's actually not recognized. I ended up having to specifically look for Java version 6 in order to use the program.

5 outdated Windows apps that are still worth using

Oldies but goodies.

7
By  Patrick Hearn

It's more convoluted than it needs to be

But it does work

With that initial setup out of the way, the VLC skin editor is functional, but it's a bit of a nightmare to use, at least by my standards. It starts by asking whether you want to open an existing theme or start from scratch, and when you open a theme, it kind of reveals how the whole thing works.

Themes are primarily composed of just nine images that contain the entire UI, but individual UI elements are defined by pointing to specific areas of the main images, composing the final look. Conceptually, it's not that hard to understand, but it's kind of difficult to work with, especially for someone like me who really isn't a designer. For me, it was much easier to just unpack the default theme and work on top of it so I have an idea of the sizes of items and all that.

The VLC skin editor does let you set sizes and starting positions for items, but it's such a convoluted mess of menus and files with confusing names that it just becomes a nightmare to align everything properly. Initially, I tried just taking the base image and doubling the resolution to then draw on top of it, but this made it very hard to adjust all the right sizes, and when I shrunk it back down, all the UI elements I had created didn't properly align with the defined areas in the theme.

It's not immediately obvious if something is wrong, either, since the default preview is the exact size of the image and doesn't account for UI elements that need to scale based on the size of the window. It was only when previewing the theme that I noticed the edges of same UI elements were caught in one of the "stretch" areas, making the UI look awful.

Windhawk makes Windows 11 beautiful, but at a great cost

System stability is invaluable until you realize its fragility.

11
By  Chandraveer Mathur

If you're a UI designer and you have some time to learn, you can probably get to grips with this and make something that looks quite cool, but for the average person, this is just too difficult.

VLC isn't made with this in mind anymore

Poor scaling and not enough support

Ultimately, I just shrunk down the UI elements a bit more and that was enough to make things work, but obviously my theme didn't look that great. Even if I had designed things correctly, basing my theme on the default one makes for a pretty disappointing experience as all the UI elements are tiny. Any buttons in the UI don't resize with the window itself, and on high-resolution screens, this UI is just hard to use.

On top of that, the VLC skin editor has received close to no love since 2007, which means it's designed for a UI that no one really uses in the first place. There's a forum thread on VideoLAN's community about the Skin Editor, and the last post is from 2024, with no one willing to take up the mantle of maintaining the tool.

I replaced three single-purpose apps with VLC's hidden features, and I haven't looked back

Our ol' reliable media player keeps some impressive tricks up its sleeve

5
By  Samarveer Singh

For VLC skins to really be worth it, the entire thing needs to be done from scratch. Skins should be based on the "modern" VLC UI and feature higher-resolution UI elements that actually work reasonably well on larger displays, and the editor itself needs to be easier to understand so you can more easily pick out what areas need to scale and which areas have permanent sizes.

Hopefully, skinning is something that's properly in the works, but if you're curious about the current iteration, you can check it out below.

VLC Skin Editor
See at VLC

I ended up having to specifically look for Java version 6 in order to use the program.

5 outdated Windows apps that are still worth using

Oldies but goodies.

7
By  Patrick Hearn

It's more convoluted than it needs to be

But it does work

With that initial setup out of the way, the VLC skin editor is functional, but it's a bit of a nightmare to use, at least by my standards. It starts by asking whether you want to open an existing theme or start from scratch, and when you open a theme, it kind of reveals how the whole thing works.

Themes are primarily composed of just nine images that contain the entire UI, but individual UI elements are defined by pointing to specific areas of the main images, composing the final look. Conceptually, it's not that hard to understand, but it's kind of difficult to work with, especially for someone like me who really isn't a designer. For me, it was much easier to just unpack the default theme and work on top of it so I have an idea of the sizes of items and all that.

The VLC skin editor does let you set sizes and starting positions for items, but it's such a convoluted mess of menus and files with confusing names that it just becomes a nightmare to align everything properly. Initially, I tried just taking the base image and doubling the resolution to then draw on top of it, but this made it very hard to adjust all the right sizes, and when I shrunk it back down, all the UI elements I had created didn't properly align with the defined areas in the theme.

It's not immediately obvious if something is wrong, either, since the default preview is the exact size of the image and doesn't account for UI elements that need to scale based on the size of the window. It was only when previewing the theme that I noticed the edges of same UI elements were caught in one of the "stretch" areas, making the UI look awful.

Windhawk makes Windows 11 beautiful, but at a great cost

System stability is invaluable until you realize its fragility.

11
By  Chandraveer Mathur

If you're a UI designer and you have some time to learn, you can probably get to grips with this and make something that looks quite cool, but for the average person, this is just too difficult.

VLC isn't made with this in mind anymore

Poor scaling and not enough support

Ultimately, I just shrunk down the UI elements a bit more and that was enough to make things work, but obviously my theme didn't look that great. Even if I had designed things correctly, basing my theme on the default one makes for a pretty disappointing experience as all the UI elements are tiny. Any buttons in the UI don't resize with the window itself, and on high-resolution screens, this UI is just hard to use.

On top of that, the VLC skin editor has received close to no love since 2007, which means it's designed for a UI that no one really uses in the first place. There's a forum thread on VideoLAN's community about the Skin Editor, and the last post is from 2024, with no one willing to take up the mantle of maintaining the tool.

I replaced three single-purpose apps with VLC's hidden features, and I haven't looked back

Our ol' reliable media player keeps some impressive tricks up its sleeve

5
By  Samarveer Singh

For VLC skins to really be worth it, the entire thing needs to be done from scratch. Skins should be based on the "modern" VLC UI and feature higher-resolution UI elements that actually work reasonably well on larger displays, and the editor itself needs to be easier to understand so you can more easily pick out what areas need to scale and which areas have permanent sizes.

Hopefully, skinning is something that's properly in the works, but if you're curious about the current iteration, you can check it out below.

VLC Skin Editor
See at VLC
item.content:encoded:
VLC is one of the most respected and well-known media players in the world, thanks to its wide range of supported formats and great performance. One thing you may not realize is that VLC actually supports skins, though only through a separate executable that can actually load skins, which looks quite different from the typical VLC interface.

And what's cool about this is that not only are there plenty of skins on VLC's website to choose from, but there's also a skin editor that's supposed to make it easier to create your own skins for VLC. I wanted to try it out, and while I like the idea, you might need a lot of patience to go through with it yourself.

VLC's skin editor is very old

It's been almost 17 years

I went into this hoping for a fun and easy way to customize my VLC experience, but things are already not very promising when you look at the release date for the latest version of the VLC skin editor. The latest version is 0.8.5, which already suggests it's somewhat unfinished, but what's really concerning is that this version was released back in 2009.

Yes, this piece of software hasn't been updated in about 17 years, and if that's not enough context, here's another way to look at it: VLC version 1.0, the first properly stable release, came out in 2010. This version of the skin editor is older than even the first official release of VLC, and there have been dozens of updates since, including two major version jumps.

It's sort of a miracle that the skin editor is still usable at all, but even then, using it will require a little bit of research. The website says you need the Java Runtime Environment version 6 or higher, but if you install the latest version available, it's actually not recognized. I ended up having to specifically look for Java version 6 in order to use the program.

5 outdated Windows apps that are still worth using

Oldies but goodies.

7
By  Patrick Hearn

It's not immediately obvious if something is wrong, either, since the default preview is the exact size of the image and doesn't account for UI elements that need to scale based on the size of the window. It was only when previewing the theme that I noticed the edges of same UI elements were caught in one of the "stretch" areas, making the UI look awful.
